claims.claim_text: nginx is the most popular web server in the world and powers about a third of websites.
claims.span_hint: section: What is a Web Server?
claims.rationale: The page directly states nginx ranks first in W3Techs' web server count and gives a roughly one-third share figure.

claims.claim_text: Docker Desktop is free for personal use and qualifying small businesses.
claims.span_hint: section: Option A > Step 1 > tool table
claims.rationale: Docker's license page directly states Docker Desktop is free for personal use and small businesses under the published thresholds.

claims.claim_text: Docker Desktop is the most popular option with the biggest community.
claims.span_hint: section: Option A > Step 1 > tool table
claims.rationale: The allowlisted sources do not provide a neutral, current source that directly verifies both 'most popular' and 'biggest community' for Docker Desktop.

claims.claim_text: OrbStack is free for personal use.
claims.span_hint: section: Option A > Step 1 > tool table
claims.rationale: OrbStack's official site is not on the allowlist, and no allowlisted source directly verifies its current personal-use pricing.

claims.claim_text: OrbStack is the fastest, lightest, and best-UX option.
claims.span_hint: section: Option A > Step 1 > tool table
claims.rationale: These are comparative performance and UX superlatives that are not directly substantiated by an allowlisted neutral source.

claims.claim_text: Podman is a daemonless container engine and supports rootless operation.
claims.span_hint: section: Option A > Step 1 > tool table (Podman Desktop row)
claims.rationale: The upstream Podman repository directly describes Podman as daemonless and documents rootless use.

claims.claim_text: Podman Desktop is free and open source.
claims.span_hint: section: Option A > Step 1 > tool table (Podman Desktop row)
claims.rationale: The project's upstream repository directly presents Podman Desktop as a free and open-source tool.

claims.claim_text: Rancher Desktop includes Kubernetes on the desktop.
claims.span_hint: section: Option A > Step 1 > tool table (Rancher Desktop row)
claims.rationale: The upstream repository directly describes Rancher Desktop as bringing Kubernetes and container management to the desktop.

claims.claim_text: The official nginx Docker image serves static content from /usr/share/nginx/html.
claims.span_hint: section: Option A > Step 5; quiz answer 3
claims.rationale: The official Docker Hub page for the nginx image directly shows static content mounted or copied into /usr/share/nginx/html.

claims.claim_text: Google Cloud offers $300 in free credit for 90 days to new customers.
claims.span_hint: section: Option B > Step 1 > provider list
claims.rationale: Google Cloud's official free-program page directly advertises $300 in free credit for 90 days.

claims.claim_text: Oracle Cloud has always-free VM resources.
claims.span_hint: section: Option B > Step 1 > provider list
claims.rationale: Oracle's official Free Tier FAQ directly lists Always Free compute offerings and states Always Free services are available for an unlimited period.

claims.claim_text: Oracle Cloud has the most generous free tier.
claims.span_hint: section: Option B > Step 1 > provider list
claims.rationale: This is a comparative superlative across vendors, and no allowlisted neutral source directly verifies it.

claims.claim_text: AWS free-tier VM eligibility changed on July 15, 2025, with older accounts using the legacy EC2 free tier and newer accounts using a different free plan.
claims.span_hint: section: Option B > Step 1 > provider list
claims.rationale: AWS's EC2 documentation directly compares benefits for accounts created before versus on or after July 15, 2025.

claims.claim_text: For AWS accounts created on or after July 15, 2025, the free-plan EC2 instance types include t3.micro, t3.small, t4g.micro, t4g.small, c7i-flex.large, and m7i-flex.large; older accounts typically see t2.micro or t3.micro.
claims.span_hint: section: Option B > Step 1 > step 2
claims.rationale: The AWS EC2 free-tier usage page directly lists the instance types by account-creation cohort.

claims.claim_text: On Ubuntu, nginx is installed and already running immediately after sudo apt install nginx -y.
claims.span_hint: section: Option B > Step 3
claims.rationale: The allowlisted Microsoft Learn guidance says the package creates a startup service but also tells the user to explicitly start nginx on first install, so it does not directly support this exact claim.

claims.claim_text: Ubuntu's nginx serves web files from /var/www/html by default.
claims.span_hint: section: Option B > Step 5; quiz answer 3
claims.rationale: Allowlisted sources show /var/www/html in Ubuntu-based examples, but none directly and cleanly verify this exact default-path claim for stock Ubuntu nginx packaging.

claims.claim_text: The first website was created by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN around 1990/1991, and info.cern.ch remains the historic first site online.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know > bullet 1
claims.rationale: The page directly identifies info.cern.ch as the first website, gives the 1990/1991 launch timeline, and shows the site in 2025.

claims.claim_text: The first web server had a handwritten note saying 'This machine is a server. DO NOT POWER IT DOWN!!'
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know > bullet 1
claims.rationale: No allowlisted source located in this run directly verifies that exact quotation.

claims.claim_text: A learner's server setup in this module is more sophisticated than the one that launched the World Wide Web.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know > bullet 1
claims.rationale: This is a subjective comparative claim rather than a sourceable factual statement.

claims.claim_text: Igor Sysoev began developing nginx in 2002 to address the C10K/high-concurrency problem.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know > bullet 2
claims.rationale: The page directly states nginx development began in 2002 and was originally developed to solve the C10k problem.

claims.claim_text: nginx uses an asynchronous event-driven architecture that performs predictably under high loads.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know > bullet 2
claims.rationale: The page directly describes nginx as using an asynchronous event-driven approach and ties that architecture to high-load performance.

claims.claim_text: With appropriate tuning, nginx can handle hundreds of thousands of simultaneous connections.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know > bullet 2
claims.rationale: No allowlisted source located in this run directly substantiates the exact 'hundreds of thousands' concurrency claim.

claims.claim_text: Netflix, Airbnb, and Dropbox all use nginx as their web server.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know > bullet 3
claims.rationale: No allowlisted source located in this run directly verifies all three company-usage claims together, or each one individually, in a current and sourceable way.

claims.claim_text: Some cloud providers only let you download the SSH key once during VM creation.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes > last row
claims.rationale: This is provider-specific behavior and no allowlisted vendor source was found that supports it as a general cloud-provider rule.

further_reading.title: A Typical HTTP Session
further_reading.why_relevant: It cleanly explains the TCP connection, request, and response flow that this module asks learners to trace.

further_reading.title: nginx Official Image on Docker Hub
further_reading.why_relevant: It is the authoritative page for the nginx container image used in the local Docker option, including the static-content path.

further_reading.title: Track Your Free Tier Usage for Amazon EC2
further_reading.why_relevant: It gives current, date-specific AWS free-plan details that matter for the cloud-server option.

notes: Focused on concrete historical, pricing, benchmark, and vendor-specific claims. Excluded analogies, exercises, code blocks, and most general teaching prose. Two spots need content revision rather than citation: OrbStack claims are effectively off-allowlist for direct support, and several Ubuntu-nginx path/startup statements are not cleanly supported by the allowlisted sources. Also note a source discrepancy: Google Cloud marketing pages say 90 days, while Google documentation pages describe 91 days.
